title: Natural forage for bees: garden and field
subtitle: Which plants provide nectar and pollen, when they flower, and how to plan a bee-friendly landscape.
excerpt: A colony of bees visits up to two million flowers to produce one jar of honey. The plants within flying range of your hives determine how well your bees thrive. This guide covers the best nectar and pollen plants by season, what a forage gap is, and how to plan planting to close it.
sourceNotes: Bumblebee Conservation Trust, Plant for Pollinators list (2024). RHS Plants for Pollinators (2024). National Bee Unit, Forage Calendar (2024). Plants for Bees, William Kirk and Francis Howes (IBRA, 2012). Wild flower habitat guidance, Natural England.

glossaryTerms.definition: A period in the year when few plants are in flower and the colony must live off its stores rather than incoming nectar. In the UK the main forage gap falls in June, often called the June gap, when spring flowers are over and summer flowers have not yet opened.

A period of abundant nectar availability, usually driven by a large area of one plant species flowering simultaneously. Oilseed rape, white clover, and lime trees are the main flows in the UK. During a strong flow, a colony can fill a super in a week.

The range of pollen types collected by a colony over the season. A wide pollen spectrum provides a more complete protein and amino acid diet for larvae. Colonies foraging on monocultures may have a narrow spectrum and poorer brood health.

content.text: Bees can fly up to three miles to find food, but they prefer shorter trips. Planting forage within half a mile of the hive has the most direct benefit. The variety of plants matters. So does when they flower. A garden full of summer lavender is wonderful in July but does nothing for a colony trying to build up in March. Good bee forage means something is flowering from February to October.

The colony needs pollen above all else in early spring. Pollen feeds the developing larvae. Before most garden flowers open, the best sources are willow (Salix species), blackthorn, hazel catkins, and pussy willow. Plant a goat willow near the apiary if you have the space; it flowers in late February and provides both pollen and nectar at a time when almost nothing else is open.
In the garden, crocus and snowdrops provide early pollen on warm days above 10°C when bees will fly short distances. Hellebores and winter aconite are also valuable. These plants will not produce a
but they keep pollen available during the colony build-up period.
Spring main flow: April to May

Fruit tree blossom, hawthorn, and oilseed rape are the three main spring flows in most of England and Wales. Oilseed rape produces a heavy nectar flow from late April. The honey sets very fast and very hard if left in the frames, so harvest it early. Hawthorn flowers in May and is one of the richest combined pollen and nectar sources in the hedgerow calendar. Dandelions, often removed as weeds, are a key early resource and should be left in any rough grass area near the apiary.

is a real challenge in areas without much white clover or early summer wildflowers. Spring blossom has finished. Summer plants have not opened. A colony at its largest population in June may be drawing on stores. Plant phacelia, borage, or cornflower specifically to bridge this gap. All three are easy annuals from seed, flower in June and July, and provide both nectar and a wide

attrs.title: White clover: the most valuable UK bee plant
attrs.body: A field or lawn with a high proportion of white clover provides one of the best sustained nectar flows in the British summer. It flowers from June to September if not cut too short. If you manage any grassland, leave areas unmown through June and July and allow clover to establish. A single acre of white clover in full flower can support a strong colony on its own.

content.text: Summer and early autumn: June to September

Lavender, lemon balm, catmint, and marjoram are among the most productive garden plants for bees by flower area. All are easy to grow and flower reliably in July and August. Lime trees (Tilia species) produce a heavy nectar flow in July where they are present. Rosebay willowherb, knapweed, and teasel are the best wild and semi-wild sources in late summer.

Late heather is the last major UK flow. White heather (Calluna vulgaris) flowers in August and September on acid moorland. Heather honey is thick and distinctive. Moving hives to heather moorland for August is practised by beekeepers in upland regions of England and Scotland.
What to plant if you have limited space

For a small garden, three plants cover most of the season well: a willow for early spring, white clover in any grass area, and lavender for summer. Add borage or phacelia as annuals to fill the June gap. Avoid double-flowered cultivars of any species; the extra petals make nectar and pollen inaccessible. Single-flowered forms are always better for bees.
